# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define sensory evaluation. List the five basic tastes recognized by the human tongue.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensory Evaluation** is the scientific discipline used to evoke, measure, analyze, and interpret human responses to food products as perceived by the five senses — sight, smell, taste, touch, and hearing. It is conducted under controlled conditions using standardized procedures to obtain objective, reproducible data from human panelists.

**The five basic tastes (with the corresponding receptor mechanism):**

| Taste | Stimulus Compound | Receptor Cell | Example Foods |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sweet** | Sugars, artificial sweeteners | T1R2/T1R3 | Sugar, honey, aspartame |
| **Sour** | Hydrogen ions (H⁺) | OTOP1 (PKD2L1) | Lemon, vinegar |
| **Salty** | Sodium ions (Na⁺) | ENaC channel | Table salt, soy sauce |
| **Bitter** | Alkaloids, polyphenols | T2R family | Coffee, quinine, broccoli |
| **Umami** | Glutamate, nucleotides | T1R1/T1R3 | MSG, tomatoes, cheese, mushrooms |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for listing the 5 tastes with examples)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between objective and subjective methods of food quality evaluation. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Objective Methods | Subjective Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **Evaluator** | Instruments / machines | Human panelists |
| **Nature of data** | Quantitative, numerical | Qualitative + quantitative |
| **Variability** | Low (reproducible) | Higher (panelist-dependent) |
| **Standards** | ISO, AOAC, FSSAI | Sensory panel protocols |
| **Cost** | High equipment, low operational | Lower setup, higher panelist cost |
| **Measurement** | Independent of human perception | Dependent on human senses |

**Examples:**

- **Objective methods:** (1) pH measurement using a pH meter, (2) Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) using a Texture Analyzer, (3) Color measurement using a Hunter Lab colorimeter, (4) Moisture content by oven-drying method.

- **Subjective methods:** (1) Hedonic rating for consumer liking, (2) Triangle test for difference detection, (3) QDA for flavor profiling, (4) Texture evaluation by mouth feel.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for tabular differentiation, 1.5 for examples)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the classification of sensory evaluation tests with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure and statistical analysis of the Triangle test used in sensory evaluation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Sensory Evaluation Tests (7 marks)**

Sensory evaluation tests are broadly classified into **three major categories** based on the type of question being answered and the type of panel used:

**1. Discriminative (Difference) Tests — (3 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Determine if a perceptible difference exists between two or more products. No preference is assessed.
- **Panel type:** Semi-trained, 25–40 panelists, screened for sensory acuity.
- **Examples:**
  - *Triangle Test:* Three samples (two identical, one different); panelist identifies the odd one. Used in QC to detect process changes.
  - *Paired Comparison Test:* Two samples; panelist selects which has more of a specified attribute. Used to verify reformulation.
  - *Duo-Trio Test:* Three samples with a reference; panelist identifies which matches the reference. Used for sensory aging studies.
  - *Two-Alternative Forced Choice (2-AFC):* Two samples; forced choice of which has more of an attribute. Highly sensitive.

**2. Descriptive Tests — (2 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Identify and quantify the intensity of specific sensory attributes (appearance, aroma, flavor, texture).
- **Panel type:** Highly trained, 8–12 panelists, with 60–100+ hours of training, validated for repeatability and panel agreement.
- **Examples:**
  - *Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA):* Panelists develop vocabulary, rate intensities on 15-cm line scales.
  - *Flavor Profile Method:* Developed by Arthur D. Little; group discussion-based profiling.
  - *Texture Profile Method:* Based on Szczesniak's classification of textural parameters.
  - *Spectrum Descriptive Analysis:* Uses anchored reference standards for absolute intensity.

**3. Affective (Preference/Acceptance) Tests — (2 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Measure consumer liking, preference, and acceptance.
- **Panel type:** Untrained consumers, 75–150+ panelists, representative of the target market.
- **Examples:**
  - *9-Point Hedonic Scale:* Most widely used; measures degree of liking.
  - *Paired Preference Test:* Forced choice of preferred sample.
  - *Acceptance Test:* Compares against a "just acceptable" threshold.
  - *Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale:* 5- or 7-point scale with "just about right" midpoint; penalty analysis reveals optimization targets.

**(b) Triangle Test — Procedure and Statistical Analysis (7 marks)**

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Sample preparation:** Three coded samples (e.g., 3-digit random numbers) are presented — two identical (A, A) and one different (B). Two orderings are used: ABB and BAA to balance position bias.
2. **Panel selection:** 25–40 semi-trained panelists, screened for sensory acuity, no allergies, no consumption of strong flavors 1 hour prior.
3. **Test conditions:** Conducted in ISO 8589 sensory booths, controlled temperature, neutral lighting (often red to mask color), 20°C.
4. **Instructions:** "Taste each sample in the order presented, from left to right. Identify the ODD sample. You must choose — guessing is required."
5. **Rinse protocol:** Water and unsalted crackers provided between samples to cleanse the palate.
6. **Data collection:** Each panelist's choice is recorded. Number of correct responses is tallied.

**Statistical Analysis (3 marks):**

The null hypothesis $H_0: p = 1/3$ (panelists are guessing) is tested against $H_1: p > 1/3$ using the binomial distribution:

$$P(X = x) = \binom{N}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{N-x}$$

With $N = 30$ panelists and $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed), the critical value is **13 correct responses** (from standard K-table).

If the observed number of correct responses ≥ 13, $H_0$ is rejected — a significant difference exists.

**Strengths:** Simple, sensitive, well-suited to QC.
**Limitations:** Does not identify the attribute causing the difference; forces guessing (some panelists may avoid guessing → use 2-AFC or forced-choice variants).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Describe the procedure for conducting a 9-point Hedonic rating test. What are its advantages and limitations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the roles of trained and consumer panels in sensory evaluation with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) 9-Point Hedonic Rating Test (7 marks)**

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Define the test objective:** Identify target consumer demographic, sample size (minimum 75), and product comparison goals.
2. **Sample preparation:** Randomly code samples with 3-digit numbers, prepare at consistent temperature (e.g., 15°C for beverages), identical portion sizes (~30 mL for liquids, ~10 g for solids).
3. **Panel recruitment:** Recruit 75–150 consumers matching the target demographic (age, gender, usage frequency, geography). Screen for food allergies and willingness to consume the product category.
4. **Test environment:** Sensory booths or a centralized consumer testing facility (central location test, CLT) with controlled conditions.
5. **Sample presentation:** Serve samples in a randomized order across panelists (to balance order and carry-over effects). Use Williams Latin Square or similar design.
6. **Rating scale explanation:** Each panelist is given the 9-point hedonic scale with verbal anchors (1 = dislike extremely, 5 = neither like nor dislike, 9 = like extremely). Written instructions are also provided.
7. **Scoring:** Each consumer rates each sample for overall liking. Optional sub-attributes (aroma, texture, flavor) may also be rated.
8. **Data analysis:** Calculate the mean hedonic score, frequency distribution, and percentage acceptance (score ≥ 6). Use ANOVA and post-hoc tests to compare samples.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- **Widely accepted** in industry and academia; easy to understand by consumers.
- **Generates both numerical and percentage data** for decision-making.
- **Sensitive to small differences** in liking.
- **No trained panel required** — uses real consumers.
- **Provides data for statistical analysis** (ANOVA, mean comparison, clustering).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Forced scale use:** Some consumers use only the middle range.
- **Cultural bias:** Scale interpretation varies across cultures.
- **Doesn't tell *why* a sample is liked/disliked** — must be paired with descriptive or JAR analysis.
- **Hedonic score ≠ purchase intent** — additional intent questions needed.

**(b) Roles of Trained and Consumer Panels (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Trained Panel | Consumer Panel |
|---|---|---|
| **Panelists** | Screened, trained for 60–100+ hours | Untrained, representative of target market |
| **Number** | 8–12 panelists | 75–150+ panelists |
| **Function** | **Detect, describe, quantify** sensory attributes | **Measure liking, preference, acceptance** |
| **Output** | Intensity scores, descriptive terms | Hedonic scores, preference percentages |
| **Test type** | Descriptive (QDA, Spectrum, Profile) | Affective (Hedonic, Preference) |
| **Cost** | Lower per session (fewer panelists) | Higher overall (large sample size) |
| **Sensory acuity** | Validated, repeatable | Variable, real-world |

**Trained Panel — Examples (3 marks):**
- *Example 1:* A dairy company wants to optimize the *creamy mouthfeel* of its yogurt. A trained panel performs **Texture Profile Analysis** to objectively measure thickness, smoothness, and spoonability across multiple formulations.
- *Example 2:* A flavor house develops a new citrus flavor. A trained panel uses **QDA** to rate citrus character, sweetness, bitterness, and aromatic notes on a 15-cm line scale, guiding the flavorist in fine-tuning.
- *Example 3:* Quality control of a beverage. A trained panel conducts a **triangle test** on production batches to detect any process-induced changes.

**Consumer Panel — Examples (3 marks):**
- *Example 1:* Before launching a new snack, the company runs a **central location test (CLT)** with 150 consumers using the **9-point hedonic scale** to assess overall liking.
- *Example 2:* A bakery chain uses an **acceptance test** to confirm that the reformulated low-sugar cake meets consumer standards (≥ 75% scoring 6+).
- *Example 3:* A pet food manufacturer uses a **JAR scale** with 100 dog owners to identify whether the aroma is "just about right," guiding product reformulation via penalty analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating significance with preference:** A significant difference (e.g., triangle test) does **NOT** mean consumers prefer one product. Always follow up with an affective test.
> 2. **Wrong panel type for wrong question:** Using a consumer panel for descriptive analysis (or vice versa) is a common, costly error.
> 3. **Ignoring carry-over effects:** In within-subject designs, residual flavor from the first sample biases the second. Always use palate cleansers and randomized order.
> 4. **Forgetting to control sensory biases:** Sample position (left vs. right), order (first vs. last), and lighting (red for color-masking) must be controlled.
> 5. **Misinterpreting pH and a_w thresholds:** Confusing "pathogen growth limit" with "spoilage limit." Pathogens stop at a_w ≈ 0.86, but *Staphylococcus aureus* tolerates a_w as low as 0.83 in high-salt environments.
> 6. **Omitting sample size justification:** Always justify panel size — for affective tests ≥ 75, for descriptive 8–12, for discriminative 25–40.
> 7. **Mixing up "flavor" with "taste":** Flavor = taste + smell + chemesthesis. Confusing these costs marks in definitions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

**1. Core Definitions (must memorize):**
- Food Quality vs. Food Safety — quality is *desirability*; safety is *harmlessness*
- Sensory Evaluation — scientific measurement of human responses to food
- Flavor = Taste + Olfaction + Chemesthesis (NOT a single sense)
- Water activity ($a_w$) — partial pressure ratio; dimensionless (0.0 to 1.0)
- HACCP — Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point

**2. Five Senses in Sensory Evaluation:**
- Vision, Olfaction, Gustation, Tactile, Audition
- Five basic tastes: Sweet, Sour, Salty, Bitter, **Umami**

**3. Three Classes of Sensory Tests:**
- **Discriminative** — difference? (Triangle, 2-AFC, Duo-Trio)
- **Descriptive** — what & how much? (QDA, Spectrum, Profile)
- **Affective** — do you like it? (Hedonic, Preference, JAR)

**4. Critical Numbers to Memorize:**
- Triangle test, N=30, α=0.05 → **13 correct responses**
- Consumer panel minimum → **75 panelists** (industry standard: 100+)
- Trained panel size → **8–12 panelists**
- Hedonic acceptance threshold → **score ≥ 6** (≥ 75% of consumers)
- Pathogen growth → $a_w > 0.91$
- *C. botulinum* inhibition → **pH < 4.6**
- Sensory booth temperature → **20 ± 2°C**
- ISO 8589 governs sensory booth design

**5. Standards & Regulations (must know):**
- **FSSAI** (India), **FDA** (USA), **EFSA** (Europe), **Codex Alimentarius** (International)
- **ISO 8589** — Sensory analysis booth design
- **ISO 5492** — Sensory analysis vocabulary
- **AOAC International** — Analytical methods
- **Big-8 Allergens** — milk, egg, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans

**6. Key Statistical Tests:**
- Triangle test → **Binomial distribution**
- Hedonic → **Mean, ANOVA, % acceptance**
- Descriptive → **Two-way ANOVA (sample × panelist)**
- Panel reliability → **Cronbach's α ≥ 0.7**
- Panelist repeatability → **CV < 20%**
- Post-hoc → **Tukey's HSD, Duncan's MRT**

**7. Critical Thresholds for Food Safety:**
- pH < 4.6 → inhibits *Clostridium botulinum* (acidic foods)
- $a_w < 0.85$ → inhibits most pathogens
- TPC acceptance limit (general) → < **$10^5$ CFU/g**
- Yeast & mold → < **$10^2$ – $10^3$ CFU/g** depending on food
- Coliform → **absent in 0.1 g** for ready-to-eat foods

**8. Practical Tips for KTU Exam:**
- Always draw a **comparison table** when differentiating methods.
- For numerical problems, **state hypotheses first, then compute, then conclude.**
- Cite **ISO/FSSAI standards** wherever applicable — adds marks.
- Use **mermaid diagrams or labeled flowcharts** to score well in 'discuss' type questions.
- For 14-mark questions, divide answer as: **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks** with sub-headings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumental Measurement of Sensory Attributes: Colour, Viscosity, Texture, Specific Gravity, Rheological and Textural Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT746 - EMERGING TECHNIQUES IN FOOD QUALITY & SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Instrumental Measurement of Sensory Attributes: Colour, Viscosity, Texture, Specific Gravity, Rheological and Textural Characteristics ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Instrumental Measurement of Sensory Attributes — Foundational Definition In food quality and safety engineering, **sensory attributes** are the perceived characteristics of a food product detected through human senses (sight, touch, taste, smell, hearing). However, human sensory panels suffer from subjectivity, fatigue, cost, and inconsistency. **Instrumental measurement** replaces or complements the human panel by providing **objective, reproducible, quantitative** data using calibrated physical and chemical instruments. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (High-Yield):** > Instrumental measurement of sensory attributes refers to the use of standardized physical instruments (colorimeters, viscometers, texture analyzers, pycnometers, rheometers) to quantify the perceived sensory properties of food — namely **Colour, Viscosity, Texture, Specific Gravity**, and their **Rheological & Textural characteristics** — in alignment with objective engineering units rather than subjective human descriptors. The five pillars covered in this module are: | Sensory Attribute | Human Sense | Instrument Family | |---|---|---| | Colour | Sight (Vision) | Colorimeter / Spectrophotometer | | Viscosity | Touch (Mouthfeel) | Viscometer / Rheometer | | Texture | Touch (Finger/Mouth) | Texture Analyzer (TPA) | | Specific Gravity | Kinesthesia (Weight perception) | Pycnometer / Hydrometer / Digital Density Meter | | Rheology/Textural characteristics | Touch / Mouthfeel | Rotational Rheometer, Texture Profiler | --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogies for Each Attribute > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Restaurant Menu Inspector"** > Imagine a food critic visiting a restaurant kitchen with a clipboard. Instead of *tasting* every dish and writing vague adjectives ("a bit thick," "nicely orange"), the inspector brings **measuring tools**: a ruler for colour intensity, a stopwatch for pour-thickness, a spring-gauge for chewiness, a weighing balance for density, and a flow-tube for pour-behavior. That is exactly what instrumental measurement does — it **translates subjective perception into engineering numbers**. - **Colour** → Like comparing paint swatches under standardized daylight (D65 illuminant) rather than guessing. - **Viscosity** → Like measuring how fast honey pours versus water through a funnel. - **Texture** → Like squeezing a stress ball and measuring how it rebounds. - **Specific Gravity** → Like floating an egg — sinking in plain water but floating in salt water tells us its relative density. - **Rheology** → Like comparing ketchup (shake → flows) vs. yogurt (needs spoon) — both are "thick" but flow differently under stress. --- ### 1.3 Why Instrumental Measurement? — The KTU Justification > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU mandates this topic because of the increasing industrial reliance on **Process Analytical Technology (PAT)**, **ISO 17025 lab accreditation**, and **HACCP/ISO 22000** quality systems — all of which demand traceable, calibrated numerical data, NOT panelist impressions. Key engineering standards referenced globally: - **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage)** — colour measurement - **ASTM D2196 / ISO 2555** — rotational viscosity - **AOAC Official Methods** — specific gravity of foods - **Bourne's TPA (1978)** — texture profile analysis ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 COLOUR — Instrumental Theory Colour perception involves three components: 1. **Light source** (illuminant — typically **D65** for daylight simulation) 2. **Object** (reflectance/transmittance properties) 3. **Observer** (standard observer with defined cone response — **CIE 1931 2°** or **1964 10°**) #### 2.1.1 CIE L\*a\*b\* (CIELAB) Color Space The most widely used uniform colour space in food analysis. $$L^* = 116 \cdot f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16$$ $$a^* = 500 \cdot \left[ f\!\left(\frac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right]$$ $$b^* = 200 \cdot \left[ f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right]$$ Where the function $f(t)$ is defined as: $$f(t) = \begin{cases} t^{1/3} & \text{if } t > \left(\frac{6}{29}\right)^3 \\[6pt] \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{29}{6}\right)^2 t + \frac{4}{29} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ - $L^*$ = Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white) - $a^*$ = Red–Green axis (+ve = red, –ve = green) - $b^*$ = Yellow–Blue axis (+ve = yellow, –ve = blue) #### 2.1.2 Total Colour Difference (ΔE\*ab) $$\Delta E^*_{ab} = \sqrt{(L^*_2 - L^*_1)^2 + (a^*_2 - a^*_1)^2 + (b^*_2 - b^*_1)^2}$$ #### 2.1.3 Hue Angle (h°) $$h^\circ = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b^*}{a^*}\right)$$ #### 2.1.4 Chroma (C\*) $$C^* = \sqrt{a^{*2} + b^{*2}}$$ #### 2.1.5 Whiteness Index (WI) — For Dairy/Flour $$WI = 100 - \sqrt{(100 - L^*)^2 + a^{*2} + b^{*2}}$$ #### 2.1.6 Browning Index (BI) — For Bakery/Meat $$BI = \frac{100 \cdot (x - 0.31)}{0.172}$$ Where $x = \dfrac{a^* + 1.75 L^*}{5.645 L^* + a^* - 3.012 b^*}$ --- ### 2.2 VISCOSITY — Instrumental Theory **Viscosity ($\eta$)** is the internal resistance of a fluid to flow, defined by Newton's law of viscosity: $$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ Where: - $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa) - $\eta$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) - $\dot{\gamma}$ = Shear rate (s⁻¹) #### 2.2.1 Kinematic Viscosity $$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho}$$ Units: **m²/s** (SI) or **centistokes (cSt)** in practice. #### 2.2.2 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids The constitutive equation generalizes to: $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ - **Newtonian**: $n = 1$ (water, milk, oils, honey at moderate shear) - **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**: $n < 1$ (ketchup, yogurt, blood) - **Dilatant (shear-thickening)**: $n > 1$ (cornstarch suspension, wet sand) - **Bingham plastic**: Requires yield stress $\tau_0$ before flow ($\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$) - **Casson fluid**: $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_c \dot{\gamma}}$ (chocolate, blood) #### 2.2.3 Apparent Viscosity $$\eta_{apparent} = \frac{\tau_{applied}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ #### 2.2.4 Capillary Viscometer — Hagen-Poiseuille Law $$\eta = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P \cdot t}{8 L V}$$ Used in **Ostwald** and **Ubbelohde** viscometers. --- ### 2.3 TEXTURE — Instrumental Theory #### 2.3.1 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) — Bourne (1978) The TPA double-compression test mimics the two-bite mastication cycle and yields **seven primary parameters**: | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Hardness | $H$ | Peak force on first compression | N (or g) | | Cohesiveness | $C$ | Ratio of positive force areas (A2/A1) | Dimensionless | | Springiness | $S$ | Distance recovered after first compression | mm | | Adhesiveness | $A$ | Negative force area during first bite | N·s | | Gumminess | $G$ | $H \times C$ | N | | Chewiness | $Ch$ | $G \times S$ | N·mm | | Resilience | $R$ | Ratio of up-stroke to down-stroke areas (first cycle) | Dimensionless | Key TPA formulas: $$Gumminess = Hardness \times Cohesiveness$$ $$Chewiness = Gumminess \times Springiness = H \times C \times S$$ #### 2.3.2 Three-Point Bend Test (for brittle foods) $$\sigma = \frac{3FL}{2bh^2}$$ $$\epsilon = \frac{6D\delta}{L^2}$$ $$E = \frac{FL^3}{4bh^3\delta}$$ Where: - $F$ = Load at break - $L$ = Support span - $b$ = Sample width - $h$ = Sample thickness - $\delta$ = Deflection - $D$ = Diameter #### 2.3.3 Shear Test (Kramer Shear Cell) Force to shear through multiple food pieces — used for vegetables and meats. --- ### 2.4 SPECIFIC GRAVITY — Instrumental Theory **Specific gravity (SG)** is the ratio of the density of a substance to the density of a reference substance (water at 4 °C for liquids, air for gases). $$SG = \frac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{reference}}$$ #### 2.4.1 Pycnometer Method $$SG = \frac{m_2 - m_1}{(m_3 - m_1) - (m_4 - m_2)}$$ Where: - $m_1$ = Empty pycnometer mass - $m_2$ = Pycnometer + sample mass - $m_3$ = Pycnometer + water mass - $m_4$ = Pycnometer + sample + water mass #### 2.4.2 Digital Density Meter (Oscillating U-Tube) $$\rho = K \cdot \tau^2 - C$$ Where $\tau$ is the oscillation period and $K$, $C$ are instrument constants calibrated with air and water. #### 2.4.3 Brix Conversion (°Brix ≈ % sucrose by weight) $$^\circ \text{Brix} \approx \text{SG} \times (0.0042 \cdot \text{SG} - 0.0034) + 0.9996$$ Or more commonly for juices: $$^\circ \text{Brix} = 261.529 \cdot \frac{SG - 1}{SG} + 0.999$$ --- ### 2.5 RHEOLOGICAL CHARACTERISTICS — Theory #### 2.5.1 Viscoelastic Models | Model | Constitutive Equation | Behavior | |---|---|---| | Maxwell | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \dfrac{\tau}{\eta}$ | Liquid-like long-term | | Kelvin-Voigt | $\tau = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Solid-like creep | | Burger's | Series Maxwell ‖ Kelvin-Voigt | Full creep + recovery | #### 2.5.2 Dynamic (Oscillatory) Rheology When sinusoidal strain $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied, the stress response is: $$\tau(t) = \tau_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$ - **Storage modulus** $G' = \dfrac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} \cos \delta$ (elastic, solid-like) - **Loss modulus** $G'' = \dfrac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} \sin \delta$ (viscous, liquid-like) - **Loss tangent** $\tan \delta = \dfrac{G''}{G'}$ - **Complex modulus** $G^* = \sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}$ **Phase angle interpretation:** - $\delta \to 0°$ → Elastic solid (gel) - $\delta \to 90°$ → Viscous liquid - $G' = G''$ → Gel point / crossover --- ### 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Quick Revision Table) | # | Parameter | Formula | SI Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Shear stress | $\tau = F/A$ | Pa | | 2 | Shear rate | $\dot{\gamma} = v/h$ | s⁻¹ | | 3 | Newton's law | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa | | 4 | Power-law fluid | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Pa | | 5 | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \eta/\rho$ | m²/s | | 6 | Hagen-Poiseuille | $\eta = \pi R^4 \Delta P t / (8LV)$ | Pa·s | | 7 | CIELAB L* | $L^* = 116 f(Y/Y_n) - 16$ | – | | 8 | ΔE\*ab | $\sqrt{(\Delta L)^2+(\Delta a)^2+(\Delta b)^2}$ | – | | 9 | Hue angle | $h^\circ = \tan^{-1}(b^*/a^*)$ | degrees | | 10 | Specific gravity | $SG = \rho_{sub}/\rho_{water}$ | dimensionless | | 11 | Pycnometer SG | $(m_2-m_1)/[(m_3-m_1)-(m_4-m_2)]$ | – | | 12 | TPA Gumminess | $H \times C$ | N | | 13 | TPA Chewiness | $H \times C \times S$ | N·mm | | 14 | Storage modulus | $G' = (\tau_0/\gamma_0)\cos\delta$ | Pa | | 15 | Loss modulus | $G'' = (\tau_0/\gamma_0)\sin\delta$ | Pa | | 16 | Loss tangent | $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ | – | | 17 | Casson model | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_c \dot{\gamma}}$ | – | | 18 | Apparent viscosity | $\eta_{app} = \tau/\dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s | --- ### 2.7 Real-World Utility - **Dairy Industry**: $L^*$ value predicts butter whiteness; $a^*$ monitors oxidation (pink defect). - **Edible Oils**: Viscosity index (VI) classifies lubricant performance. - **Bakery**: TPA chewiness correlates with staling; $G'$/$G''$ crossover predicts bread freshness. - **Juice Industry**: °Brix (refractometer) directly tied to sugar content and pricing. - **Chocolate Manufacture**: Casson plastic viscosity + yield stress is a quality passport. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hagen-Poiseuille Law for Capillary Viscometry We start with the **Navier-Stokes equation** for steady, laminar, incompressible, fully-developed flow through a cylindrical tube of radius $R$: $$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv}{dr} \right) = \frac{1}{\eta} \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$ **Step 1:** Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate once: $$r \frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} r^2 + C_1$$ **Step 2:** Apply the no-slip boundary condition: at $r = R$, $v = 0$. First, divide by $r$: $$\frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} r + \frac{C_1}{r}$$ For the velocity to remain finite at $r = 0$, we must have $C_1 = 0$. Integrate again: $$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)$$ **Step 3:** This is the parabolic **Poiseuille velocity profile**. The volumetric flow rate $Q$ is found by integrating $v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$ over the cross-section: $$Q = \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$ **Step 4:** Since $Q = V/t$ (volume $V$ discharged in time $t$), solving for $\eta$ gives the working equation of any capillary viscometer: $$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P \cdot t}{8 L V}}$$ **Conversion to kinematic viscosity** divides by density: $$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage and Loss Modulus from Complex Compliance Start with complex notation. Apply a complex strain $\gamma^*(t) = \gamma_0 e^{i\omega t}$, generating a complex stress $\tau^*(t) = \tau_0 e^{i(\omega t + \delta)}$. **Step 1:** Define the complex modulus: $$G^* = \frac{\tau^*(t)}{\gamma^*(t)} = \frac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} e^{i\delta}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Euler's identity $e^{i\delta} = \cos\delta + i \sin\delta$: $$G^* = \frac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} \cos\delta + i \frac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} \sin\delta$$ **Step 3:** Identify real and imaginary parts: $$G' = \frac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0}\cos\delta \quad ; \quad G'' = \frac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0}\sin\delta$$ **Step 4:** Magnitude (the "stiffness" at that frequency): $$|G^*| = \sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}$$ **Step 5:** Phase angle (the ratio of viscous to elastic response): $$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — CIELAB L\* from Tristimulus Y The CIE L* formula was derived to be **perceptually uniform** — equal numerical differences in L\* correspond to equal perceived lightness differences. Starting from the cube-root nonlinearity intended to linearize the Munsell Value scale: **Step 1:** Define a normalized stimulus function: $$f(t) = t^{1/3} \quad \text{for } t > \left(\tfrac{6}{29}\right)^3$$ **Step 2:** For small $t$, a linear tangent is matched at the breakpoint to ensure continuity of the first derivative: $$f(t) = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{29}{6}\right)^2 t + \frac{4}{29} \quad \text{for } t \leq \left(\tfrac{6}{29}\right)^3$$ **Step 3:** The lightness L* is then defined so that: - $Y/Y_n = 1$ (perfect white) → $L^* = 100$ - $Y/Y_n = 0$ (perfect black) → $L^* = 0$ $$L^* = 116 \cdot f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16$$ **Step 4:** Verify the black point. At $t = 0$: $f(0) = 4/29$, so $L^* = 116 \cdot (4/29) - 16 = 16 - 16 = 0$. ✓ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A capillary viscometer has $R = 0.5 \text{ mm}$, $L = 10 \text{ cm}$. A honey sample flows through under $\Delta P = 200 \text{ Pa}$ and takes 45 s to discharge $V = 5 \text{ mL}$. Given the density of honey is $\rho = 1420 \text{ kg/m}^3$, calculate the dynamic and kinematic viscosity. **Step 1 — Unit conversion:** $$R = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$ $$V = 5 \text{ mL} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into Hagen-Poiseuille:** $$\eta = \frac{\pi (5 \times 10^{-4})^4 \cdot 200 \cdot 45}{8 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\eta = \frac{\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \cdot 9000}{4 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\eta = \frac{1.767 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.42 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s} \approx 0.442 \text{ mPa·s}$$ Wait — let me recompute carefully: $$R^4 = (5 \times 10^{-4})^4 = 625 \times 10^{-16} = 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^4$$ $$\pi R^4 \Delta P t = 3.14159 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 200 \times 45 = 1.767 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$8LV = 8 \times 0.10 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\eta = \frac{1.767 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.42 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 3 — Kinematic viscosity:** $$\nu = \frac{4.42 \times 10^{-4}}{1420} = 3.11 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 0.311 \text{ cSt}$$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right | Penetrometer | Lactometer (hydrometer) | | Fruit juice | Spectrophotometer | Ostwald capillary | – | Refractometer (°Brix) | | Honey | Hunter Lab | Falling-ball (Höppler) | Penetrometer | Pycnometer | | Chocolate melt | – | Casson-fit rotational | – | – | | Bread / cake | L\*a\*b\* colorimeter | – | TPA + 3-point bend | Volume displacement | | Meat / fish | Hunter L\*a\*b\* | – | Warner-Bratzler shear | – | | Sauces, ketchup | – | Brookfield rotational | Back-extrusion | Density cup | | Powdered spices | Munsell / spectrophotometer | – | – | Tapped density | | Yogurt | – | Bob/cup rotational | Back-extrusion | Pycnometer | | Edible oil | Lovibond / tintometer | Ostwald (kinematic) | – | Digital density meter | --- ### 3.6 Analytical Decision Tree for Viscosity ``` START │ ├── Liquid (flows without threshold)? │ YES → Newtonian or non-Newtonian? │ │ │ │ │ ├── Newtonian (water, oil, milk) → Capillary OR rotational │ │ └── Non-Newtonian (ketchup, paste) → Rotational, sweep shear rate │ NO → Solid-like / gel? │ │ │ ├── Weak gel (jelly, pudding) → Oscillatory, find G' = G'' crossover │ └── Strong gel / dough → Creep test, find J(t) compliance END ``` ## SECTION 4: Structural [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **Define CIELAB colour space. State the significance of L\*, a\*, b\* values in food quality analysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** CIELAB (CIE 1976 L\*a\*b\*) is a perceptually uniform, device-independent colour space developed by the Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage. It expresses any colour using three coordinates: 1. **L\*** — Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white). In food analysis, it measures whiteness of milk, flour, rice, or lightness of meat. 2. **a\*** — Red–green axis (+ve red, –ve green). Used to monitor meat browning, tomato redness, and chlorophyll loss in vegetables. 3. **b\*** — Yellow–blue axis (+ve yellow, –ve blue). Tracks yellowing of oils, ripening of fruits, and Maillard browning. Significance: CIELAB allows **objective numerical comparison** of food colour independent of lighting/viewer, enabling quality control, batch-to-batch consistency, and shelf-life studies. The ΔE\*ab quantifies perceptible colour change. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **Differentiate between Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluids with two food examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Newtonian | Non-Newtonian | |---|---|---| | Viscosity behaviour | Constant with shear rate | Varies with shear rate | | Constitutive equation | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ or similar | | Flow index | $n = 1$ | $n \neq 1$ | | Examples | Water, milk, refined oils, air | Ketchup, yogurt, blood, chocolate, cornstarch slurry | | Measurement | Single viscosity value sufficient | Flow curve required | **Newtonian food examples:** (i) Water (η ≈ 1 mPa·s at 20 °C), (ii) Refined sunflower oil (η ≈ 50 mPa·s). **Non-Newtonian food examples:** (i) Ketchup (shear-thinning pseudoplastic), (ii) Cornstarch suspension in water (shear-thickening dilatant). --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **principle, components, and working of a tristimulus colorimeter** for food colour measurement. Describe the CIELAB colour space with all three equations and state any **four derived parameters** used in food analysis. (7 marks) **(b)** A juice sample has $L^* = 42.3$, $a^* = 18.5$, $b^* = 35.2$. A reference standard has $L^* = 45.0$, $a^* = 16.0$, $b^* = 32.0$. Compute the **total colour difference (ΔE\*ab)**, the **chroma (C\*)**, and the **hue angle (h°)**. Comment on whether the difference is visually perceptible. (7 marks) --- ##### **Model Solution for (a):** **Principle (2 marks):** A tristimulus colorimeter measures colour by illuminating the sample with a standardized light source (typically a pulsed xenon arc simulating D65 daylight), collecting the reflected light through **three silicon photodiodes** filtered to mimic the CIE standard observer's X (red), Y (green), and Z (blue) response curves. **Components (2 marks):** 1. **Illumination system** — Tungsten-halogen or xenon lamp + D65 filter. 2. **Integrating sphere** — Coats the inside with BaSO₄ to provide uniform diffuse illumination. 3. **Sample port** — Glass slide or cup holder for liquid/solid samples. 4. **Detector assembly** — Three filtered photodiodes (X, Y, Z) or a single diode with rotating filter wheel. 5. **Microprocessor** — Converts raw signals to X, Y, Z tristimulus values then to L\*a\*b\*. 6. **Display** — LCD/LED showing numerical values. **Working (1 mark):** The sample is placed at the port. Light reflects off the sample, is captured by the integrating sphere, filtered into X/Y/Z components, and the microprocessor applies the CIELAB transformation equations to output L\*, a\*, b\*. **CIELAB Equations (1 mark):** $$L^* = 116 \cdot f(Y/Y_n) - 16, \quad a^* = 500[f(X/X_n) - f(Y/Y_n)], \quad b^* = 200[f(Y/Y_n) - f(Z/Z_n)]$$ where $f(t) = t^{1/3}$ if $t > (6/29)^3$, else linear branch. **Four derived parameters (1 mark):** 1. **ΔE\*ab** — total colour difference. 2. **C\*** — chroma (saturation). 3. **h°** — hue angle. 4. **WI** — Whiteness Index (dairy/flour) or **BI** — Browning Index (bakery/meat). --- ##### **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Compute ΔL\*, Δa\*, Δb\*:** $$\Delta L^* = 42.3 - 45.0 = -2.7$$ $$\Delta a^* = 18.5 - 16.0 = +2.5$$ $$\Delta b^* = 35.2 - 32.0 = +3.2$$ **Step 2 — Apply the ΔE\*ab formula:** $$\Delta E^*_{ab} = \sqrt{(-2.7)^2 + (2.5)^2 + (3.2)^2}$$ $$= \sqrt{7.29 + 6.25 + 10.24} = \sqrt{23.78}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta E^*_{ab} \approx 4.88}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Chroma C\* (use sample values):** $$C^* = \sqrt{(18.5)^2 + (35.2)^2} = \sqrt{342.25 + 1239.04} = \sqrt{1581.29}$$ $$\boxed{C^* \approx 39.77}$$ **Step 4 — Compute hue angle h°:** $$h^\circ = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{35.2}{18.5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.9027)$$ $$\boxed{h^\circ \approx 62.27^\circ}$$ **Step 5 — Perceptibility comment:** According to standard perceptibility thresholds: - ΔE < 1 → not perceptible - 1 ≤ ΔE < 2 → barely perceptible - 2 ≤ ΔE < 3.5 → noticeable on close inspection - ΔE > 3.5 → clearly perceptible difference - ΔE > 5 → strong difference Since **ΔE\*ab ≈ 4.88 > 3.5**, the colour difference between the sample and the reference standard is **clearly perceptible** to the average human observer. The juice shows higher redness (+2.5) and yellowness (+3.2) but lower lightness (−2.7) compared to the standard, indicating it is **darker, redder, and more yellow** — possibly an over-ripe or over-processed batch. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** What is **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)**? With a neat labelled force–time diagram, explain how **Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, and Chewiness** are determined instrumentally. (7 marks) **(b)** A sauce exhibits **power-law behaviour** with consistency index $K = 12 \text{ Pa·s}^n$ and flow behaviour index $n = 0.45$. It is sheared at $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$. Calculate the **shear stress**, the **apparent viscosity**, and comment on whether this is a **shear-thinning** or **shear-thickening** fluid. (7 marks) --- ##### **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition (1 mark):** Texture Profile Analysis (TPA), developed by **Bourne (1978)**, is an instrumental double-compression test that imitates the human two-bite mastication cycle. A probe is driven into a food sample twice in succession while force, distance, and time are recorded, yielding a force–time curve from which seven textural parameters are derived. **Force–time curve (2 marks) — Diagram to be drawn:** ``` Force (N) ▲ │ H (Hardness) │ ╱╲ │ ╱ ╲ H₂ │ ╱ ╲ ╱╲ │ ╱ A1 ╲ ╱ ╲ │ ╱ ╲ ╱ A2 ╲ │ ╱ ╲╱ ╲___ │ ╱ ← Adhesiveness→ │ ╱ withdrawal ╱ │ ╱ ╱ Springiness (S) │ ╱ ╱ ───► │╱____________________╱_________________► Time (s) └─Compression 1─ Pause ─Compression 2─ ``` **Parameter definitions (4 marks):** | Parameter | Definition | How measured from curve | |---|---|---| | **Hardness (H)** | Peak force of first compression | Vertical height of first peak | | **Cohesiveness (C)** | Ratio of work in second bite to first | $C = A_2 / A_1$ (dimensionless, 0–1) | | **Springiness (S)** | Distance recovered between bites | Horizontal distance at force zero between bites | | **Adhesiveness (A)** | Negative work during withdrawal | Area of negative force loop | | **Gumminess (G)** | Energy required to disintegrate a semi-solid | $G = H \times C$ | | **Chewiness (Ch)** | Energy for chewing a solid | $Ch = G \times S = H \times C \times S$ | --- ##### **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - $K = 12 \text{ Pa·s}^n$ - $n = 0.45$ - $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$ **Step 1 — Shear stress using power-law model:** $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ $$\tau = 12 \cdot (50)^{0.45}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $(50)^{0.45}$:** $$\ln(50) = 3.912$$ $$0.45 \times 3.912 = 1.7604$$ $$e^{1.7604} = 5.815$$ So $(50)^{0.45} \approx 5.815$ $$\tau = 12 \times 5.815 = 69.78 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Apparent viscosity:** $$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{69.78}{50} = 1.396 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** Since $n = 0.45 < 1$, the fluid is a **shear-thinning pseudoplastic** fluid. This means: - As shear rate increases, viscosity decreases. - At low shear (rest), the sauce appears thick (high $\eta_{app}$). - At high shear (squeezing bottle, stirring), it flows easily. - Typical examples: ketchup, mustard, salad dressings, banana purée. **Confirmation by apparent viscosity behaviour:** if we had computed at a higher $\dot{\gamma}$, say 200 s⁻¹, $\eta_{app}$ would drop to: $$\tau = 12 \times 200^{0.45} = 12 \times 11.43 = 137.2 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\eta_{app} = 137.2 / 200 = 0.686 \text{ Pa·s} < 1.396 \text{ Pa·s} \;\;\checkmark$$ This drop confirms **shear-thinning**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Sign errors in Δa\* and Δb\*** — Always subtract REFERENCE from SAMPLE consistently. Reversing the order changes ΔE sign but not magnitude — but writing `ΔL = 45.0 − 42.3 = +2.7` is a **1-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. > 2. **Hue angle quadrant mistake** — `tan⁻¹(b*/a*)` gives an angle in only ONE quadrant. KTU expects students to note when `a* < 0` and add 180°, or when `a* < 0 and b* < 0` add 360°. Skipping this loses **1 mark**. > 3. **Mixing up units** — Viscosity is **Pa·s**, NOT **Pa** (which is shear stress). Confusing them loses **1–2 marks**. > 4. **TPA Gumminess** — A common error is computing Gumminess = $H + C$ instead of $H \times C$. This is a **2-mark deduction** as it shows conceptual misunderstanding. > 5. **Pycnometer formula memorization** — Students often forget the denominator `(m3 − m1) − (m4 − m2)` and incorrectly write `(m3 − m1) + (m4 − m2)`. Lose **1 mark**. > 6. **Forgetting to state temperature** — Specific gravity is temperature-dependent. Reporting SG without stating "at 20 °C" or "at 25 °C" loses **0.5 mark**. > 7. **Power-law units** — Students write $K$ in units of Pa·sⁿ without realizing $n$ depends on K's exponent; forgetting the units makes answer technically wrong. Lose **0.5–1 mark**. > 8. **ΔE perceptibility scale** — Students often state ΔE = 4.88 is "not perceptible" — WRONG. Memorize: <1 not, 1–2 barely, 2–3.5 noticeable, >3.5 clearly visible. Lose **1 mark**. --- ### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density bullet recap — read this 5 minutes before the exam!** #### **A. Colour Measurement** - [ ] CIELAB uses D65 illuminant + 10° observer (most common) or 2°. - [ ] L\* = 0 (black) to 100 (white). - [ ] a\* positive = red, negative = green. - [ ] b\* positive = yellow, negative = blue. - [ ] ΔE\*ab = √[(ΔL)² + (Δa)² + (Δb)²]. - [ ] Chroma = √(a² + b²); Hue = tan⁻¹(b\*/a\*). - [ ] Munsell system uses Hue + Value + Chroma (H V/C). - [ ] Whiteness Index uses only L\* and b\*; Browning Index for bakery. #### **B. Viscosity Measurement** - [ ] η = τ / γ̇ (Newton's law). - [ ] Kinematic ν = η / ρ. - [ ] Ostwald/Ubbelohde use Hagen-Poiseuille equation. - [ ] Brookfield rotational gives shear rate sweep → flow curve. - [ ] Power-law: τ = K γ̇ⁿ. - [ ] Newtonian: n = 1. Pseudoplastic: n < 1. Dilatant: n > 1. - [ ] Bingham plastic has yield stress τ₀. - [ ] Casson model fits chocolate. - [ ] SI unit is **Pa·s**; **1 cP = 1 mPa·s**. #### **C. Texture Measurement** - [ ] TPA = two-bite simulation (Bourne 1978). - [ ] Hardness = peak force of 1st compression. - [ ] Cohesiveness = A2/A1. - [ ] Springiness = distance recovered. - [ ] Gumminess = Hardness × Cohesiveness. - [ ] Chewiness = Gumminess × Springiness = H × C × S. - [ ] Adhesiveness = negative area. - [ ] Resilience = upstroke/downstroke area ratio. - [ ] Warner-Bratzler shear for meat toughness. - [ ] Three-point bend for brittle foods (cookies, chips). #### **D. Specific Gravity Measurement** - [ ] SG = ρ_substance / ρ_water (at 4 °C reference). - [ ] Pycnometer most accurate for viscous/expensive samples. - [ ] Hydrometer (lactometer, saccharometer) for liquids. - [ ] Digital density meter uses oscillating U-tube. - [ ] Brix ≈ % sucrose; refractometer measures °Brix. - [ ] Density cup for paints/sauces; not as accurate. - [ ] Always report temperature (typically 20 °C or 25 °C). #### **E. Rheological Characteristics** - [ ] Viscoelastic behavior combines solid (elastic) + liquid (viscous). - [ ] G' = storage modulus (elastic energy stored). - [ ] G'' = loss modulus (viscous energy dissipated). - [ ] tan δ = G''/G'. - [ ] Gel: G' > G'' (δ → 0). - [ ] Liquid: G'' > G' (δ → 90°). - [ ] Gel point: G' = G'' (δ = 45°). - [ ] Creep test: J(t) = γ(t)/τ₀ compliance. - [ ] Models: Maxwell (relaxation), Kelvin-Voigt (creep), Burger's (combined). #### **F. Exam Strategy Quick Tips** - [ ] Always write **units** next to numerical answers. - [ ] State **assumptions** (laminar flow, isothermal, Newtonian/non-Newtonian). - [ ] Draw **labelled diagrams** for TPA curves — these guarantee full marks. - [ ] Cite **CIE/ASTM/AOAC standards** when relevant — shows depth. - [ ] Convert all units to **SI** before substituting into formulas. - [ ] When in doubt, mention **CIE D65, 10° observer, ASTM D2196, Bourne 1978, AOAC 920.212** — these are KTU-favourite reference codes. --- **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — INSTRUMENTAL MEASUREMENT OF SENSORY ATTRIBUTES**

topics.title: Use of Rheological Properties in Food Science and Engineering, Texture Profile Analysis, Sensory Evaluation Using Artificial Intelligence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT746 - EMERGING TECHNIQUES IN FOOD QUALITY & SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Use of Rheological Properties in Food Science and Engineering, Texture Profile Analysis, Sensory Evaluation Using Artificial Intelligence # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Rheological Properties in Food Science and Engineering ### Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition **Food Rheology** is the scientific discipline that studies the deformation and flow behavior of food materials under applied stress or strain. It quantifies how food materials respond to mechanical forces, characterized primarily by **viscosity** ($\eta$), **elastic modulus** ($G$), and **loss modulus** ($G''$). Rheological properties bridge the gap between sensory perception and instrumental measurement, enabling food engineers to predict processability, texture, shelf-life, and consumer acceptance. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Rheology is NOT merely viscosity. It encompasses the entire viscoelastic spectrum — from pure solids (elastic) to pure liquids (viscous). Food materials are predominantly **viscoelastic** systems. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of food rheology like the behaviour of different household substances: - **Water** → Flows easily when you tilt the glass (Newtonian liquid, constant viscosity) - **Honey** → Flows slowly, thick (viscous Newtonian) - **Tomato Ketchup** → Stays in the bottle, but rushes out when shaken hard (shear-thinning, non-Newtonian) - **Toothpaste** → Won't flow until you squeeze hard enough (Bingham plastic with yield stress) - **Bread Dough** → Stretches like rubber then breaks (viscoelastic) In food engineering, instead of "feeling" these behaviours with your hands, we use **rheometers** to measure them precisely — much like a thermometer quantifies temperature rather than relying on touch. --- ## 1.2 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) ### Formal Definition **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** is an instrumental, double-compression (two-bite) test that mimics the mastication process by compressing a food sample twice using a texturometer, generating a **force–time (or force–distance) curve** from which seven primary textural parameters are quantitatively derived. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine biting a piece of cheese twice in succession: - **First bite (compression 1):** How hard did your teeth press? → **Hardness** - **Did the cheese stick to your palate?** → **Adhesiveness** - **Did the cheese spring back to its original shape before the second bite?** → **Springiness** - **Did the second bite require less force?** → **Cohesiveness** - **Did it feel gummy or chewy?** → **Gumminess / Chewiness** TPA simply puts numbers on what your tongue and teeth experience subjectively. --- ## 1.3 Sensory Evaluation Using Artificial Intelligence ### Formal Definition **AI-based Sensory Evaluation** refers to the application of artificial intelligence — including machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), electronic sensing systems (e-nose, e-tongue), and computer vision — to objectively replicate, predict, and model human sensory perception (taste, smell, appearance, texture, sound) of food products. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a "robot sommelier" that: - **Sees** the colour, gloss, and shape of a wine (computer vision) - **Smells** the bouquet through chemical sensors (e-nose) - **Tastes** acidity, bitterness, and sweetness through electrochemical sensors (e-tongue) - **Learns** from thousands of human tasters' scores (ML models) - **Predicts** how a new wine will be rated by a sommelier This replaces the slow, expensive, inconsistent human panel with a fast, reproducible AI system — without entirely eliminating the human element. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Principle:** AI does not REPLACE human senses; it AUGMENTS them by providing consistent, high-throughput, multi-dimensional data that complements traditional sensory panels. ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard TPA test speed:** **1 mm/s** (crosshead speed) - **Standard compression ratio:** **75%** of original sample height (often 30–75%) - **Standard temperature:** **25°C** (or product-specific) - **E-nose response time:** typically **10–60 seconds** - **AI model accuracy threshold:** **> 0.85** (for sensory prediction, R² ≥ 0.85) --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Food Materials by Rheological Behaviour Foods are classified based on how they respond to applied shear stress ($\tau$) and shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$): ### Operational Logic Steps 1. **Apply controlled shear** to the food sample in a rheometer 2. **Measure the response** (flow or deformation) 3. **Plot $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$** — the curve shape reveals the material class 4. **Fit mathematical models** to derive rheological constants 5. **Correlate with process parameters** (pumping, mixing, extrusion) --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Rheological Models > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM GOLD:** Memorize the model equations, the meaning of each parameter, and which foods fit which model. ### Table 2.1: Master Rheological Models & Equations | **Model Name** | **Governing Equation** | **Parameters** | **Typical Food Examples** | |---|---|---|---| | **Newtonian** | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) | Water, milk, honey, edible oils | | **Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele)** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ), $n$ = flow behaviour index (dimensionless) | Fruit purees, sauces, syrups | | **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_{0}$ = yield stress (Pa), $\eta_{p}$ = plastic viscosity (Pa·s) | Toothpaste, margarine, ketchup | | **Herschel–Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $\tau_{0}$ (Pa), $K$ (Pa·sⁿ), $n$ (dimensionless) | Mayonnaise, chocolate, dough | | **Casson** | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\tau_{0}$, $\eta_{\infty}$ = infinite-shear viscosity | Chocolate melt, molten cheese | | **Carreau** | $\dfrac{\eta - \eta_{\infty}}{\eta_{0} - \eta_{\infty}} = \left[1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^{2}\right]^{\frac{n-1}{2}}$ | $\eta_{0}$ = zero-shear viscosity, $\eta_{\infty}$, $\lambda$ = time constant (s), $n$ | Polymer melts, concentrated dairy | > [!NOTE] > **Power Law Special Cases:** > - $n = 1$ → reduces to Newtonian (with $K = \eta$) > - $n < 1$ → **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** — most foods > - $n > 1$ → **shear-thickening (dilatant)** — rare (e.g., starch suspensions) --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — TPA Parameters ### Table 2.2: The Seven Classical TPA Parameters | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Derivation from Force–Time Curve** | **Unit** | **Sensory Correlate** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Hardness** | $H$ | Peak force of first compression ($F_{1}$) | N (or g) | Force required to bite through | | **Adhesiveness** | $A$ | Negative work of first bite (area $A_{3}$ below baseline) | N·s (or g·s) | Stickiness to mouth/teeth | | **Springiness** | $S$ | $\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}$ (time between peaks ratio) | dimensionless | Recovery of original shape | | **Cohesiveness** | $C$ | $\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}$ (2nd area / 1st area) | dimensionless | Internal bond strength | | **Gumminess** | $G$ | $H \times C$ | N (or g) | Energy needed to disintegrate (semi-solids) | | **Chewiness** | $Ch$ | $H \times C \times S$ | N (or g) | Energy to chew (solids) | | **Resilience** | $R$ | $\dfrac{A_{5}}{A_{4}}$ (upstroke area / downstroke area, 1st bite) | dimensionless | Immediate elastic recovery | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — AI Sensory Models ### Table 2.3: AI Methods Used in Sensory Evaluation | **AI Method** | **Application** | **Equation / Concept** | **Output Metric** | |---|---|---|---| | **PCA (Principal Component Analysis)** | Dimensionality reduction of sensor arrays | $Z = X \cdot W$ (projection matrix) | Explained variance ratio | | **LDA (Linear Discriminant Analysis)** | Classifying sensory categories | Maximizes between-class / within-class variance | Classification accuracy | | **ANN (Artificial Neural Network)** | Predicting sensory scores from instrumental data | $y = f\left(\sum_{i} w_i x_i + b\right)$ | $R^{2}$, RMSE | | **SVM (Support Vector Machine)** | Taste/aroma classification | Hyperplane: $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Accuracy, F1-score | | **CNN (Convolutional NN)** | Visual quality (appearance) | Convolution + pooling layers | Top-1/Top-5 accuracy | | **Random Forest** | Predicting consumer acceptance | Ensemble of decision trees | $R^{2}$, MAE | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Performance Metrics:** > - **RMSE** (Root Mean Square Error) = $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}}{n}}$ > - **R² (Coefficient of Determination)** = $1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}}{\sum (y_{i} - \bar{y})^{2}}$ > - **Acceptance threshold for AI sensory models:** $R^{2} \geq 0.85$ and $RMSE \leq 0.5$ (on 9-point hedonic scale) --- ### Real-World Utility in Food Engineering 1. **Pipe & Pump Sizing:** Apparent viscosity governs pumping power (Hagen–Poiseuille relationship). 2. **Heat Exchanger Design:** Rheology affects residence time and fouling. 3. **Extrusion Cooking:** Flow behaviour index $n$ predicts extrudate expansion. 4. **Quality Control:** TPA parameters predict shelf-life stability of bakery products. 5. **Product Development:** AI sensory systems enable rapid prototyping without large consumer panels (saves **~70%** of sensory cost). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Apparent Viscosity from Power Law Model **Problem Context:** A fruit puree is tested in a concentric cylinder rheometer. At a shear rate of $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$, the measured shear stress is $\tau = 12 \text{ Pa}$. The flow behaviour index is $n = 0.45$, and consistency index $K$ is unknown. Determine $K$ and the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the governing equation (Power Law):** $$ \tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for $K$ by substitution:** $$ 12 \text{ Pa} = K \cdot (50 \text{ s}^{-1})^{0.45} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $\dot{\gamma}^{n}$:** $$ (50)^{0.45} = e^{0.45 \cdot \ln(50)} = e^{0.45 \cdot 3.912} = e^{1.7605} \approx 5.815 $$ **Step 4 — Isolate $K$:** $$ K = \frac{12}{5.815} \approx 2.064 \text{ Pa·s}^{n} $$ **Step 5 — Define apparent viscosity:** $$ \eta_{\text{app}} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} $$ **Step 6 — Evaluate at $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$:** $$ \eta_{\text{app}} = 2.064 \cdot (100)^{0.45 - 1} = 2.064 \cdot (100)^{-0.55} $$ **Step 7 — Compute $(100)^{-0.55}$:** $$ (100)^{-0.55} = 10^{-1.10} \approx 0.0794 $$ **Step 8 — Final value:** $$ \eta_{\text{app}} \approx 2.064 \cdot 0.0794 \approx 0.164 \text{ Pa·s} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** Since $n = 0.45 < 1$, the puree is **shear-thinning**. Doubling the shear rate from 50 → 100 s⁻¹ decreased the apparent viscosity by ~32% — critical for designing pumps and pasteurizers. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: TPA Parameter Calculation from a Force–Time Curve **Problem Context:** A cylindrical gel sample (height = 20 mm, diameter = 30 mm) is subjected to TPA at 75% compression. From the recorded force–time curve: - $F_{1} = 18 \text{ N}$ (peak of 1st compression) - $F_{2} = 8 \text{ N}$ (peak of 2nd compression) - $T_{1} = 4.0 \text{ s}$ (time to 1st peak) - $T_{2} = 3.0 \text{ s}$ (time to 2nd peak) - Area under 1st down-stroke $A_{1} = 45 \text{ N·s}$ - Area under 2nd down-stroke $A_{2} = 18 \text{ N·s}$ - Negative area (adhesiveness) $A_{3} = 1.5 \text{ N·s}$ Calculate all 7 TPA parameters. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Hardness:** $$ H = F_{1} = 18 \text{ N} $$ **Step 2 — Adhesiveness:** $$ A = A_{3} = 1.5 \text{ N·s} $$ **Step 3 — Springiness:** $$ S = \frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = \frac{3.0}{4.0} = 0.75 \text{ (dimensionless)} $$ **Step 4 — Cohesiveness:** $$ C = \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}} = \frac{18}{45} = 0.40 \text{ (dimensionless)} $$ **Step 5 — Gumminess:** $$ G = H \times C = 18 \times 0.40 = 7.2 \text{ N} $$ **Step 6 — Chewiness:** $$ Ch = G \times S = 7.2 \times 0.75 = 5.4 \text{ N} $$ **Step 7 — Resilience:** Assume $A_{4}$ (1st down-stroke up to max) $= 20 \text{ N·s}$ and $A_{5}$ (1st up-stroke) $= 8 \text{ N·s}$: $$ R = \frac{A_{5}}{A_{4}} = \frac{8}{20} = 0.40 $$ ### Interpretation - Moderate hardness (18 N) suggests a **firm gel** - Low cohesiveness (0.40) indicates the gel is **crumbly** (weak internal bonds) - Springiness of 0.75 means it recovers **75%** of its height — **good elastic recovery** - Chewiness 5.4 N confirms the gel requires **moderate chewing effort** --- ## 3.3 Derivation: AI Model Performance for Sensory Prediction **Problem Context:** An ANN predicts overall acceptability scores (9-point hedonic scale). For $n = 10$ test samples: | Actual $y_{i}$ | 7 | 6 | 8 | 5 | 9 | 4 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 5 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Predicted $\hat{y}_{i}$ | 6.8 | 6.2 | 7.5 | 5.4 | 8.6 | 4.5 | 7.1 | 5.9 | 7.7 | 4.8 | Calculate **RMSE** and **R²**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute residuals $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$:** $$ [0.2,\ -0.2,\ 0.5,\ -0.4,\ 0.4,\ -0.5,\ -0.1,\ 0.1,\ 0.3,\ 0.2] $$ **Step 2 — Square residuals:** $$ [0.04,\ 0.04,\ 0.25,\ 0.16,\ 0.16,\ 0.25,\ 0.01,\ 0.01,\ 0.09,\ 0.04] $$ **Step 3 — Sum of squared residuals (SSR):** $$ \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = 0.04 + 0.04 + 0.25 + 0.16 + 0.16 + 0.25 + 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.09 + 0.04 = 1.05 $$ **Step 4 — Mean of actual values:** $$ \bar{y} = \frac{7+6+8+5+9+4+7+6+8+5}{10} = \frac{65}{10} = 6.5 $$ **Step 5 — Total Sum of Squares (SST):** $$ \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = (0.5)^2 + (-0.5)^2 + (1.5)^2 + (-1.5)^2 + (2.5)^2 + (-2.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (-0.5)^2 + (1.5)^2 + (-1.5)^2 $$ $$ \text{SST} = 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 + 6.25 + 6.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 = 22.5 $$ **Step 6 — RMSE:** $$ \text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{\text{SSR}}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.05}{10}} = \sqrt{0.105} \approx 0.324 $$ **Step 7 — R²:** $$ R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{1.05}{22.5} = 1 - 0.0467 = 0.953 $$ **Interpretation:** With $R^{2} = 0.953$ and $\text{RMSE} = 0.324$ (well below 0.5), the ANN exhibits **excellent predictive capability** for sensory acceptability — exceeding the KTU-recommended threshold of $R^{2} \geq 0.85$. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram A: Classification of Food Materials by Rheological Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Food Material Subjected to Stress]:::startNode --> B{Behaviour Type?} B -->|Purely Viscous| C[Newtonian Flow] B -->|Viscoelastic| D[Non-Newtonian Flow] B -->|Purely Elastic| E[Hookean Solid] C --> C1[Water, Milk, Honey, Edible Oils] C --> C2[tau = eta times gamma-dot] D --> D1{Time-Independent?} D --> D2{Time-Dependent?} D1 --> F1[Shear-Thinning] D1 --> F2[Shear-Thickening] D1 --> F3[Yield Stress Fluids] F1 --> F1A[Fruit Purees, Sauces, Yogurt] F1 --> F1B[n LESS THAN 1 Power Law] F2 --> F2A[Starch Suspensions, Wet Sand] F2 --> F2B[n GREATER THAN 1 Power Law] F3 --> F3A[Bingham: Ketchup, Toothpaste] F3 --> F3B[Herschel-Bulkley: Mayonnaise] F3 --> F3C[tau-0 plus K gamma-dot to n] D2 --> G1[Thixotropic: Yogurt, Paints] D2 --> G2[Rheopectic: Rare in Food] E --> E1[Brittle Foods, Hard Candy] classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Diagram B: TPA Force–Time Curve Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Start: Probe Contact]:::startNode --> DS1[Down-Stroke 1
Compression] DS1 --> P1[Peak Force F1
Hardness = F1] P1 --> US1[Up-Stroke 1
Negative Area = Adhesiveness] US1 --> WS[Wait Period
Springiness Recovery] WS --> DS2[Down-Stroke 2
Compression] DS2 --> P2[Peak Force F2] P2 --> US2[Up-Stroke 2
End of Test] DS1 -.->|Area A1| CALC[Calculation Block] DS2 -.->|Area A2| CALC US1 -.->|Area A3| CALC CALC --> OUT[Output Parameters:
H, A, S, C, G, Ch, R] classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram C: AI-Based Sensory Evaluation Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB INPUT[Sensory Input Sources]:::inputNode INPUT --> A1[Electronic Nose] INPUT --> A2[Electronic Tongue] INPUT --> A3[Computer Vision System] INPUT --> A4[Spectroscopic Sensors NIR FTIR] A1 --> PRE[Pre-Processing
Noise Filter, Normalization] A2 --> PRE A3 --> PRE A4 --> PRE PRE --> FEAT[Feature Extraction
PCA, LDA, Wavelets] FEAT --> ML[Machine Learning Models
ANN, SVM, Random Forest] ML --> PRED[Prediction Block] PRED --> OUT1[Sensory Score Prediction] PRED --> OUT2[Quality Classification] PRED --> OUT3[Shelf-Life Estimation] PRED --> OUT4[Consumer Acceptance Forecast] VAL[Human Sensory Panel
Ground Truth]:::validate --> COMP[Model Validation
R-squared RMSE Confusion Matrix] PRED --> COMP COMP --> FB[Feedback Loop
Model Retraining] classDef inputNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef validate fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram D: Comparison — Instrumental Texture vs. Sensory Texture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Instrumental TPA] --> A1[Hardness] A --> A2[Cohesiveness] A --> A3[Springiness] A --> A4[Adhesiveness] B[Human Sensory Panel] --> B1[Hardness Perception] B --> B2[Cohesiveness Perception] B --> B3[Springiness Perception] B --> B4[Sticky Texture] A1 <-->|Correlation| B1 A2 <-->|Correlation| B2 A3 <-->|Correlation| B3 A4 <-->|Correlation| B4 CORR[Pearson Correlation r GREATER THAN 0.7
indicates Valid Correlation] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define food rheology. List the four main rheological models commonly applied to food systems with one food example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Food rheology is the study of deformation and flow of food materials under applied stress, bridging the gap between food structure and mechanical behaviour. | Model | Food Example | |---|---| | Newtonian | Milk / Honey / Water | | Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele) | Fruit purees / Tomato sauce | | Bingham Plastic | Toothpaste / Ketchup | | Herschel–Bulkley | Mayonnaise / Chocolate | *(0.5 mark for definition, 0.25 mark each × 4 = 1.0 mark for listing, 1.0 mark for correct examples and equations)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)? Name any four textural parameters determined by TPA.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** TPA is a double-compression instrumental test that mimics mastication, generating a force–time curve from which multiple textural parameters are calculated. **Four key parameters:** 1. **Hardness** — peak force of first compression 2. **Cohesiveness** — ratio of areas (2nd/1st compression) 3. **Springiness** — recovery between compressions 4. **Adhesiveness** — negative work of first bite (stickiness) *(1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for parameters)* --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Discuss in detail the application of rheological properties in food science and engineering. Describe the Power Law model with its mathematical form and explain the significance of the flow behaviour index $n$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat sketch of a force–time curve, explain how the seven textural parameters are derived in Texture Profile Analysis (TPA). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Application of Rheological Properties (7 marks)** **1. Process Design (2 marks):** - Rheological data governs **pipe sizing, pump selection, and heat exchanger design**. - Apparent viscosity determines **pumping power** using Hagen–Poiseuille law. - Power Law constants ($K$, $n$) are critical inputs for **CFD simulation** of food processing. **2. Quality Control (2 marks):** - Rheology correlates with **texture, spreadability, pourability**, and **mouthfeel**. - E.g., chocolate must have specific yield stress for proper coating; ice cream must have specific viscosity for extrusion. **3. Product Development (1.5 marks):** - New formulations (low-fat, sugar-free) require rheological matching with originals. - E.g., fat replacers must replicate the **pseudoplastic behaviour** of full-fat mayonnaise. **4. Shelf-Life Prediction (1.5 marks):** - Rheological changes (e.g., gel syneresis, crystallization) indicate **structural breakdown** over time. **Power Law Model (included within the 7 marks):** $$ \tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n} $$ - **$K$** (consistency index) — viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 1 \text{ s}^{-1}$; higher $K$ = thicker food - **$n$** (flow behaviour index): - $n = 1$ → Newtonian - $n < 1$ → Shear-thinning (most foods) - $n > 1$ → Shear-thickening - $n = 0$ → Plastic solid --- **(b) TPA Curve & Parameters (7 marks)** **Sketch Description (2 marks):** The TPA curve is a force–time plot showing two compression cycles. Peak 1 ($F_1$) defines hardness; the dip after Peak 1 represents adhesiveness; Peak 2 ($F_2$) is the second compression peak. Area $A_1$ and $A_2$ are under the first and second compression curves respectively. **Seven Parameters (5 marks — 0.7 each):** 1. **Hardness ($H$)** = $F_1$ (peak force of first bite) 2. **Adhesiveness ($A$)** = Negative area after first bite ($A_3$) 3. **Springiness ($S$)** = $T_{2}/T_{1}$ 4. **Cohesiveness ($C$)** = $A_{2}/A_{1}$ 5. **Gumminess ($G$)** = $H \times C$ 6. **Chewiness ($Ch$)** = $H \times C \times S$ 7. **Resilience ($R$)** = Upstroke area / Downstroke area (1st bite) --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the principle and components of an **electronic nose (e-nose)** and **electronic tongue (e-tongue)** used in AI-based sensory evaluation of food. Compare their working mechanisms. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the role of **machine learning algorithms** (ANN, SVM, PCA) in modelling and predicting sensory attributes of food. Discuss the performance metrics used to validate these models. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) E-Nose and E-Tongue (7 marks)** **E-Nose — Principle (2 marks):** Mimics the human olfactory system. Detects volatile organic compounds (VOCs) using an array of non-specific chemical sensors. Pattern recognition via AI classifies aromas. **Components (1.5 marks):** 1. **Sensor array** — Metal oxide semiconductors (MOS), conducting polymers, quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) 2. **Data acquisition system** — Converts sensor signals to digital 3. **Pattern recognition engine** — ANN, PCA **E-Tongue — Principle (2 marks):** Mimics human gustation. Detects non-volatile taste compounds (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami) via electrochemical methods. **Components (1.5 marks):** 1. **Sensor array** — Potentiometric, voltammetric, or impedimetric electrodes 2. **Multichannel signal processor** 3. **AI classifier** — LDA, SVM, ANN **Comparison (integrated above):** E-nose → gases/VOCs; E-tongue → liquid-soluble tastants. --- **(b) Machine Learning in Sensory Modelling (7 marks)** **PCA — Principal Component Analysis (1.5 marks):** Reduces high-dimensional sensor data (e.g., 100 sensors) to 2–3 principal components while preserving maximum variance. Used for **visualization** and **dimensionality reduction**. **SVM — Support Vector Machine (1.5 marks):** Supervised classifier that finds the optimal hyperplane separating sensory categories (e.g., fresh vs. spoiled). Effective for **binary and multi-class** problems. **ANN — Artificial Neural Network (2 marks):** Multi-layer perceptron learns nonlinear mapping between instrumental measurements and sensory scores. Architecture: input layer (sensor features) → hidden layers (nonlinear) → output (sensory score). **Performance Metrics (2 marks):** - **RMSE** (Root Mean Square Error) — measures prediction error magnitude - **R²** (Coefficient of Determination) — proportion of variance explained - **Confusion Matrix** — for classification (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) - **Acceptance threshold:** $R^{2} \geq 0.85$ for publication-grade sensory AI --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Adhesiveness with Cohesiveness** — Adhesiveness is the negative area (sticky); Cohesiveness is the ratio of areas (internal bonds). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this. > 2. **Writing $n = 1$ for Power Law means "viscous"** — Actually, $n = 1$ makes it Newtonian. Most foods have $n < 1$. > 3. **Forgetting units** — Hardness is in **N** (not mm or s). Chewiness = N (not N·s). > 4. **Mixing up E-nose and E-tongue** — E-nose detects **VOCs (gases)**; E-tongue detects **taste molecules (liquids)**. > 5. **Calculating R² incorrectly** — Use SSR/SST correctly; SST is based on $\bar{y}$, not zero. > 6. **Skipping the sketch in TPA questions** — Always include a labelled force–time curve; **2 marks reserved** for diagram. > 7. **Not mentioning double compression** — TPA is fundamentally a **two-bite test**; single compression is just puncture test. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist #### Rheology Essentials - ✅ Food rheology = study of **deformation + flow** under stress - ✅ **Newtonian:** $\tau = \eta\dot{\gamma}$ (water, milk, honey) - ✅ **Power Law:** $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^{n}$; $n < 1$ = shear-thinning - ✅ **Bingham:** yield stress + linear flow (ketchup) - ✅ **Herschel–Bulkley:** yield stress + power law (mayonnaise) - ✅ **Apparent viscosity:** $\eta_{\text{app}} = \tau/\dot{\gamma} = K\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ - ✅ Applications: **pump design, mixing, extrusion, quality control** #### Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) Essentials - ✅ TPA = **double-compression test** (two-bite simulation) - ✅ Seven parameters: **H, A, S, C, G, Ch, R** - ✅ **Hardness** = $F_1$ - ✅ **Cohesiveness** = $A_2/A_1$ - ✅ **Springiness** = $T_2/T_1$ - ✅ **Gumminess** = $H \times C$ (semi-solids) - ✅ **Chewiness** = $H \times C \times S$ (solids) - ✅ Standard compression: **75%**, speed: **1 mm/s**, temp: **25°C** - ✅ Always draw the **force–time curve** in exam answers #### AI Sensory Evaluation Essentials - ✅ **E-nose** → detects **VOCs** (aroma), uses MOS sensors - ✅ **E-tongue** → detects **tastants**, uses electrochemical sensors - ✅ **PCA** → dimensionality reduction - ✅ **SVM** → classification (fresh/spoiled) - ✅ **ANN** → nonlinear regression for sensory scores - ✅ **CNN** → image-based appearance quality - ✅ Performance metrics: **R² ≥ 0.85, RMSE ≤ 0.5** - ✅ AI **augments**, does not replace, human sensory panels - ✅ Cost saving: **~70%** vs. traditional panels #### Cross-Cutting Concepts - ✅ Rheology ↔ Texture ↔ Sensory are **interconnected** - ✅ Instrumental measurements (rheometer, TPA) must **correlate** with sensory panel data (Pearson $r \geq 0.7$ for validity) - ✅ AI models require **ground truth** from human panels for training and validation - ✅ All three techniques together enable **objective food quality assessment** --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a question asks "Discuss the application," always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) Principle → (3) Mathematical model with equation → (4) At least 3 food examples → (5) Engineering significance**. This 5-step structure is what KTU examiners reward with full marks.

topics.title: Application of Computer Vision Systems for Objective Assessment of Food Qualities: Evaluation of Color, Texture, Shape and Size
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT746 - EMERGING TECHNIQUES IN FOOD QUALITY & SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application of Computer Vision Systems for Objective Assessment of Food Qualities: Evaluation of Color, Texture, Shape and Size

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Computer Vision Systems (CVS) in Food Quality

A **Computer Vision System (CVS)** is a non-destructive, automated optical inspection framework that replicates the human visual interpretation process by acquiring digital images of a food sample, processing them through algorithms, and extracting quantifiable features (color, texture, shape, size) for objective quality grading, defect detection, and freshness assessment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Computer vision is an engineering discipline that deals with the computational extraction of meaningful information from images and video frames. In food technology, it is used as a rapid, hygienic, repeatable, and non-contact method to replace subjective sensory panels with **quantitative, sensor-derived metrics**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Robot Food Inspector"

Imagine a **highly trained quality inspector** standing at the end of a conveyor belt in a food factory. Every fruit, biscuit, or chicken breast that passes by is judged by the inspector on four traits:

- **Color** → "Is this apple red enough to sell?"
- **Texture** → "Does the surface of this bread look too rough?"
- **Shape** → "Is this French fry straight, or is it bent?"
- **Size** → "Is this fish fillet the right weight category?"

Now, replace the human inspector with a **camera + computer**. The **camera acts as the eye** (image acquisition), the **computer acts as the brain** (image processing), and the **algorithms act as the decision-making logic** (feature extraction). The key advantage? The robot inspector **never gets tired, never has a bad day, and always judges by the exact same rule book**. This is the essence of Computer Vision in food quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Building Blocks of any Food CVS:**
> 1. **Illumination Chamber** (standardized lighting, usually LED)
> 2. **Image Sensor** (CCD/CMOS camera)
> 3. **Frame Grabber / Digitizer** (A/D conversion)
> 4. **Image Processing Unit** (software algorithms)
> 5. **Decision / Sorting Actuator** (rejection or grading mechanism)

## 1.3 Why Objective Assessment Matters

Subjective sensory evaluation suffers from **human variability (bias, fatigue, panel disagreement)**. CVS provides:

- **Repeatability:** Same pixel = same number, every time.
- **Throughput:** Inspect 100–10,000 items/minute.
- **Hygiene:** No human contact (critical for meat, dairy).
- **Data Traceability:** Digital record for HACCP and FSMA compliance.

| Aspect | Human Inspector | Computer Vision System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Speed** | Slow (~30 items/min) | Very fast (~10,000 items/min) |
| **Consistency** | Variable (tired/bored) | Constant |
| **Measurement Type** | Qualitative (Likert scale) | Quantitative (exact RGB values) |
| **Hygiene Risk** | High (direct contact) | Zero contact |
| **Cost** | Recurring (salary) | One-time capital |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Food CVS

A typical computer vision system executes the following sequential logic:

1. **Sample Placement & Illumination:** The food item is placed in a controlled lighting enclosure (typically diffuse LED ring light at **D65 standard illuminant**, 6500 K) to eliminate shadows and reflections.
2. **Image Acquisition:** A CCD/CMOS camera captures the reflected/transmitted light and converts it into a 2D matrix of pixels, where each pixel has intensity values (e.g., 0–255 for 8-bit).
3. **Pre-processing:** Noise removal (Gaussian filtering), background segmentation (Otsu's thresholding), and color space conversion.
4. **Feature Extraction:** Mathematical computation of color, texture, shape, and size parameters.
5. **Classification:** Use of statistical models (PCA, LDA) or machine learning (ANN, SVM) to map features to quality grades.
6. **Decision Output:** Pneumatic/solenoid actuator for sorting (Accept / Reject).

## 2.2 Evaluation of Color

Color is the **most dominant quality cue** in food acceptance. It is quantified by converting RGB (camera-native) into perceptually uniform color spaces like **CIELAB (L\*a\*b\*)** or **HSV**.

- **L\*** = Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
- **a\*** = Green–Red axis (−a = green, +a = red)
- **b\*** = Blue–Yellow axis (−b = blue, +b = yellow)
- **H (Hue)** = Dominant wavelength (0–360°)
- **S (Saturation)** = Color purity (0–100%)
- **V (Value)** = Brightness (0–100%)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Total Color Difference (ΔE\*ab)** is the most widely used single-number indicator of color change in food. ΔE\*ab < 1 is "not perceptible", < 3 is "acceptable", > 5 is "clearly different".

## 2.3 Evaluation of Texture

Texture describes the **spatial distribution of gray-level intensities** on a food surface. Three mathematical approaches dominate:

- **Statistical methods:** Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) — features like Contrast, Correlation, Energy, Homogeneity.
- **Structural methods:** Edge frequency, run-length statistics.
- **Model-based methods:** Fractal dimension, Markov Random Fields.

## 2.4 Evaluation of Shape

Shape features describe the **geometric boundary** of the food object. The most common descriptors are:

- **Area (A):** Number of enclosed pixels × calibration factor²
- **Perimeter (P):** Sum of boundary pixels
- **Circularity (C):** How close the object is to a perfect circle
- **Aspect Ratio (AR):** Major axis ÷ Minor axis
- **Roundness, Solidity, Compactness**

## 2.5 Evaluation of Size

Size is measured after **camera calibration**, where the pixel-to-millimeter ratio is established using a reference object (e.g., a calibration grid). Key metrics include:

- **Feret diameter (max & min):** Distance between parallel tangents
- **Major axis length, Minor axis length**
- **Equivalent diameter:** Diameter of a circle with the same area as the object

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Master Formula Table (exam-ready)**

| Domain | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
